ch1-scene0: A sepia-tinged image of a young man (Nick Carraway) sitting at an antique writing desk, pen poised thoughtfully over a journal. Behind him are tall bookshelves cluttered with old volumes, and a sunlit window lets in diffuse morning light. The decor is 1920s American, with dark wood, framed family portraits, and a sense of quiet introspection. Gentle shadows convey a mood of reflective calm.
ch1-scene1: A small, weather-beaten bungalow stands among overgrown grass and wildflowers at the tip of West Egg, with a glimpse of the Long Island Sound in the background. The light is bright and summery, with sunlight dappling the yard. A modest, used Dodge car is parked out front, and a distant view of Gatsby's opulent mansion sits nearby with towers and manicured lawns. A Finnish housekeeper can be glimpsed through the window, busy with kitchen duties. The mood is both humble and expectant.
ch1-scene2: Nick Carraway approaches an elaborate red-and-white Georgian Colonial mansion set on sweeping green lawns that stretch down to the bay. Early evening light bathes the scene in gold, with flowering gardens and brick walks. Tom Buchanan stands confidently on the front porch in stylish riding clothes, legs apart, surveying the estate. The air is breezy, and the sense of wealth and old money pervades the atmosphere.
ch1-scene3: A sumptuous, rosy-colored living room bathed in late afternoon light streaming through wide, open French windows. Breezy white curtains billow dreamily, and an enormous sofa dominates the center, upon which Daisy Buchanan and Jordan Baker recline in elegant white dresses. Tom Buchanan and Nick Carraway enter from the hallway, with Nick captivated by the scene and Tom moving restlessly. The furnishings reflect Jazz Age opulence: chandeliers, ornate rugs, and delicate period details. The mood is lush, languid, and faintly surreal.
ch1-scene4: A glowing, open-air porch set for dinner at dusk, candlelight flickering on a linen-covered table. Daisy and Jordan sit side by side, fresh in white summer dresses and pearls, while Tom and Nick, dressed in evening suits, settle in. The garden beyond is dark with approaching twilight. Silverware glints, and the scene has the elegance and understated tension of fashionable 1920s society. The air is tinged with summer, and the atmosphere is at once sociable and constrained.
ch1-scene5: Softly-lit indoor rooms connect to a deep, shadowed veranda outside. Daisy and Nick sit on a wicker settee beneath the gloom of the porch, nearly silhouetted by garden darkness, while Tom and Jordan are seen through a window, seated at opposite ends of a long couch in a lamp-lit room. The overall effect is one of intimacy and emotional distance, with the soft glow of a lamp, reflective surfaces, and hints of luxurious Jazz Age decor. The night is warm and muffled, with a mood of honest revelation and quiet sorrow.
ch1-scene6: The entrance to the Buchanan mansion is aglow with cheerful electric light spilling through an open doorway. Nick stands just beyond the threshhold, hands in pockets, while Tom and Daisy stand close together in the light, both elegantly attired in fashionable 1920s evening wear. Daisy, in a beaded dress, turns to call after Nick, her gesture graceful. Tom stands stolidly at her side. The moment is brief, posed, and tinged with both warmth and detachment.
ch1-scene7: Nighttime drapes Nick's modest bungalow, with pale moonlight shimmering on the lawn and the silent, massive shape of Gatsby's mansion looming fifty yards away. Nick sits pensively on an old grass roller, shadows shifting around him, as a solitary, mysterious figure (Gatsby) stands on the distant lawn gazing toward the dark water. The mansion's windows glimmer faintly. Over the bay, a faint green light glows in the darkness. The scene is quiet, stirring, and melancholic, capturing the elusive promise and loneliness of the Jazz Age.
ch2-scene0: A 1920s American scene in the Valley of Ashes between West Egg and New York: bleak, desolate landscape of gray dust and drifting ash, dotted with grotesque, ash-formed ridges, hills, and faint outlines of houses and chimneys. Dim, powdery air veils a handful of ash-gray, weary men in work clothes with leaden spades, stirring up clouds near a crawling line of old gray cars and railroad tracks. Dominating the scene, high above the valley, loom the faded, gigantic blue eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg on a timeworn billboard, framed in enormous yellow spectacles, watching over the solemn dumping ground. At the edge of the wasteland stands a small yellow-brick block with three shops: a shabby garage marked “George B. Wilson. Cars bought and sold. Repairs,” an all-night restaurant approached by a trail of ashes, and an empty storefront. Foreground: Tom Buchanan, imposing in a tan suit, brisk hat, and slicked hair, forcibly guiding the narrator—Nick Carraway, slighter build, neat gray suit and hat—over a low whitewashed fence toward the garage door. The mood is grim, the palette cold and muted, period-accurate clothing and vehicles, with subtle hints of Jazz Age urban decay.
ch2-scene1: A dingy, sparsely furnished garage cluttered with the debris of used automobile parts and a single dust-covered Ford in the corner. George Wilson stands just outside his small office, nervously wiping his hands on a rag. Tom Buchanan enters confidently with Nick Carraway trailing him. The light is dim, filtered through grimy windows, casting a drab gray over the bleak room. Myrtle Wilson, faintly stout and dressed in a blue crêpe-de-chine dress, appears on the stairs, her presence radiating vitality. The atmosphere is tense, the furniture worn, and the visuals strictly 1920s working-class.
ch2-scene2: A bleak roadside beneath the stern gaze of the Doctor T. J. Eckleburg billboard, where Tom, Nick, and Myrtle pause, keeping out of sight. The gray dust coats everything. In the foreground, an Italian child in ragged clothes arranges torpedoes on the track, absorbed in his task. Tom and Nick stand nearby in their city attire of the early 1920s, Myrtle poised in her worn dress, her movements anxious. The light is overcast, reinforcing the scene’s melancholy.
ch2-scene3: On the crowded platform of a New York train station, Myrtle, now dressed in a tightly fitting brown muslin dress, stands alongside Tom and Nick. The newsstand is busy with early 1920s city dwellers in hats and coats. Myrtle buys magazines as Tom watches, and the group bustles through the station, across the echoing drive above, in search of a taxi. The sunlight glows through the urban haze, illuminating bustling traffic and period taxicabs along city streets.
ch2-scene4: A lavender taxicab with grey upholstery is halted curbside on a lively 1920s Fifth Avenue. Through the open taxi window, a gray-haired dog seller—stooped, weathered, and in a faded suit—offers a basket of wriggling puppies to Myrtle, who sits inside the cab with anticipation. Tom is at her side, and Nick observes the exchange from the far seat. Patches of bright afternoon sunlight fall on the group, the city bustling in the background with pedestrians in flapper dresses and straw boaters.
ch2-scene5: Outside a stately, white 1920s apartment building on 158th Street, Myrtle—clutching her new puppy, magazines, and shopping bags—leads Tom and Nick toward the entrance. The neighborhood shows signs of upper Manhattan quiet and respectability. Inside, they ride a small elevator, Myrtle standing proudly at the front, Tom and Nick behind her, the elevator boy visible at the controls. The lighting is golden and bright, clear afternoon streaming into the foyer.
ch2-scene6: A cramped, over-decorated top-floor apartment living room, filled with heavy tapestried furniture featuring Versailles scenes, and dominated by an over-enlarged photograph of a matronly woman. Myrtle, fussing over her new puppy and purchases, is changing dresses with theatrical purpose. Nick and Tom look on while the elevator boy brings in a straw box and milk for the dog. The sunlight pours through the windows, filling the room with a warm, late afternoon glow, illuminating the haphazard clutter of magazines, bottles, and Town Tattle.
ch2-scene7: A crowded 1920s apartment living room where Myrtle, now dressed in an elaborate cream-colored chiffon dress, sits regally as guests stream in. Catherine, striking in a fashionable red bob and white powdered face, enters with jangling pottery bracelets. Mr. McKee stands near a wall with a respectful demeanor, a pale man with shaved cheeks, while his wife—sharp and languid—lounges by his side. Tom and Nick mingle, glasses of whiskey in hand. The puppy rests drowsily in a patch of sunlight on the rug. The room is buzzing with nervous energy, laughter, and the scents of perfume and cigarettes.
ch2-scene8: Crowded and smoky, the apartment is filled with revelers. Myrtle dominates, sweeping about in cream-colored chiffon, her manner grand. Catherine sits with Nick on the couch, leaning in to gossip, while the McKees converse nearby—Mr. McKee taking in the furnishings, Mrs. McKee posing with pride. Tom stands by a locked sideboard, pouring whiskey for the guests. Sunlight fades as the golden afternoon turns into smoky twilight, with lamps casting long shadows. Town Tattle magazines scatter across tables, and the dog lounges listlessly.
ch2-scene9: As night deepens, the air is thick with cigarette smoke and the signs of excess. The room is visibly more disheveled; Mr. McKee has fallen asleep, slumped awkwardly in a chair, his hands clutching his lap. Myrtle sits on a couch, loudly telling stories and laughing, the dress askew. Nick, dazed, sits apart, half-listening to the ongoing arguments. Catherine and Mrs. McKee are wreathed in conversation, drinks in hand. The dog lies on the table, listless, while sandwich remnants and spilled drinks clutter surfaces. Lamp light glows warmly across the scene, city darkness pressing at the windows.
ch2-scene10: Tension fills the overcrowded room under yellowed lamp light. Myrtle stands defiantly, blood streaming from her nose as Tom towers over her, hand just lowered from the blow. Mrs. McKee and Catherine rush to Myrtle’s aid, scolding and consoling, while Myrtle collapses onto the couch, grasping a Town Tattle magazine. Mr. McKee, groggy, stares at the scene as he makes his way unsteadily to the door. The dog cowers on the table. The chaotic, cramped atmosphere is heightened by scattered belongings, loud voices, and a sense of aftermath.
ch2-scene11: Nick and Mr. McKee stand awkwardly beside an old-fashioned elevator, its doors open and the elevator boy visible at the controls, frowning and in uniform. Mr. McKee, still disheveled, makes his dignified exit. Briefly, the scene shifts to McKee’s stark bedroom, where he sits on his bed in his underwear, a large art portfolio open on his lap, under harsh overhead light. The tone is surreal and bleary, just before dawn.
ch2-scene12: A cavernous, deserted Pennsylvania Station in the small hours—long shadows, grand marble columns, and a handful of weary travelers. Nick sits on a bench in his rumpled suit, newspaper in hand, dozing as he waits for the four o’clock train. Faint pre-dawn light filters through high windows, casting blue-grey reflections across the vast, empty space. The scene is quiet, lonely, and drained after the previous night’s chaos.
ch3-scene0: A grand 1920s Long Island mansion glitters with hundreds of colored lights as evening falls. The blue gardens overflow with elegantly dressed men and women in evening attire—flapper dresses, tuxedos, bobbed hair, and beaded headbands. Some guests lounge on beach chairs by the sand, others dance on the lit-up lawn, and servers in smart uniforms pass with trays of cocktails. Motorboats can be glimpsed on the Sound beyond. The air is lively with celebration and the vibrant energy of the Jazz Age.
ch3-scene1: In the morning light beside a small bungalow, Nick Carraway, dressed casually, receives a formal envelope from Gatsby’s chauffeur, who wears a robin’s-egg blue uniform. The manicured grounds of West Egg stretch out behind them, hinting at the grand estate next door. The mood is quiet and anticipatory.
ch3-scene2: Early evening in the vast gardens of Gatsby’s estate. Nick, in crisp white flannels, stands awkwardly among swirling crowds of guests wearing vibrant 1920s party dresses and smart suits. Tables overflow with food and drinks, and lanterns hang from the trees. The atmosphere is dazzling with movement and color, a sea of unfamiliar faces against the backdrop of Gatsby’s illuminated mansion.
ch3-scene3: Twilight softens the marble steps of Gatsby’s mansion. Jordan Baker in a sleek beaded dress stands at the top, surveying the party. Nick ascends toward her, while below, two young women in matching yellow dresses flit by. Waiters circulate with cocktails. The scene radiates wealth, style, and mildly strained social energy.
ch3-scene4: At a round garden table surrounded by lanterns, Nick and Jordan sit with the girls in yellow, Lucille in an elaborate evening gown, and three indistinct men, all introduced as 'Mr. Mumble.' Everyone is engrossed in lively, conspiratorial conversation. The party’s glow shines in the background, emphasizing an air of intrigue and speculation.
ch3-scene5: A long banquet table under trees draped with lights, where elegant guests—mainly married couples from East Egg and Jordan’s undergraduate escort—dine amidst elaborate silver settings and overflowing platters. The crowd is more restrained, radiating a sense of exclusivity and mild condescension among the festivities.
ch3-scene6: A high-ceilinged library lined with dark, carved English oak bookshelves. Owl Eyes, middle-aged and stout with huge round spectacles, perches drunkenly on the edge of a polished table, gesturing excitedly at books. Nick and Jordan—her in an elegant evening dress, Nick attentive—stand facing him. Everything is lit by golden lamplight, emphasizing the room’s opulence and scholarly aura.
ch3-scene7: The brightly lit canvas dance floor in Gatsby’s garden is crowded with couples swirling energetically—men in tuxedos, women in vibrant flapper dresses. The orchestra plays, and performers—a celebrated tenor, a notorious contralto, and the stage twins (girls in yellow)—entertain the buzzing crowd. Champagne flows in giant glasses, and the whole scene glows silver beneath a high summer moon, a tableau of revelry and Jazz Age excess.
ch3-scene8: At a small table on Gatsby’s moonlit lawn, Nick sits with Jordan, a young gentleman (Gatsby), and a lively, laughing little girl. The party glimmers in the background, but the table feels momentarily secluded. Jay Gatsby, impeccably dressed, is turned toward Nick, his expression open and inviting. The moment is subtly intimate amidst the festivities.
ch3-scene9: By the marble staircase of Gatsby’s mansion, Gatsby pauses, turning away as a butler in formal uniform summons him toward the house. Nick and Jordan stand nearby under the party’s colored lanterns, watching Gatsby go. Groups of guests mingle in the semi-darkness, laughter still echoing around the garden.
ch3-scene10: On the outdoor stage, the orchestra leader in tails announces to the crowd with grand gestures. Orchestra members, including Vladmir Tostoff at the piano, ready their instruments. Guests gather to listen, faces expectant, as colorful lights illuminate the scene. Gatsby, apart at the edges, watches approvingly.
ch3-scene11: Jordan, in her evening gown, rises from a garden table as Gatsby’s butler leans in discreetly to deliver Gatsby’s invitation. Nick remains seated, slightly surprised. Around them, the party is thinning, the lights softer now as the night deepens. The house glows in the background.
ch3-scene12: Inside an opulent ballroom with tall windows overlooking the terrace, one girl in yellow plays the piano while a red-haired young woman from the chorus stands beside her, singing passionately, her cheeks streaked with inky tears. Other guests, couples and actors in various stages of drunkenness and distress, fill the room. Art Deco sconces and moonlight heighten the sense of exhausted revelry.
ch3-scene13: Gatsby, elegant and composed, and Jordan, graceful and pensive in her shimmering dress, emerge from the grand library into the wide, bright foyer of the mansion. Other guests collect their hats and coats, forming small clusters near the open front doors. Outside on the porch, members of the East Egg party beckon as the evening comes to a close.
ch3-scene14: In the deep night outside Gatsby’s mansion, the headlights of numerous cars cast stark shadows on a new coupé stuck in a ditch, one wheel gone. Chauffeurs and guests crowd around, some in formal evening wear, others in chauffeur uniforms. Owl Eyes, dazed in round spectacles, stands next to the man in a rumpled duster; another pale, dangling individual emerges unsteadily from the car. The mood is chaotic and surreal, horns blaring while the revelry ebbs.
ch3-scene15: Nick, in his white flannels, cuts across Gatsby’s silent lawn. Behind him, Gatsby stands alone on the grand porch, one hand raised in a formal gesture of farewell. The once-glittering house now glows softly, almost empty, with moonlight and leftover echoes of laughter casting an air of stately isolation over the scene.
ch3-scene16: Nick walks briskly along the bustling, skyscraper-lined streets of 1920s Manhattan, passing bond offices and dark restaurants teeming with young men in business suits. He is sometimes alone, sometimes in conversation, always with a sense of urban restlessness and late-day golden light casting long shadows over the scenes. Fifth Avenue and 33rd Street, the Yale Club, and the Pennsylvania Station all appear in flashes as Nick wanders, solitary but observant.
ch3-scene17: Daytime at a grand country house in Warwick. Nick and Jordan sit together outdoors, she poised with insouciance in a light summer dress, he in a blazer and tie. Golf bags rest nearby. The mood is conversational—intimate but charged—as they discuss driving. The lush, green setting and period leisurewear suggest the exclusive, idle lives of the privileged class.
ch1-scene8: A close, lively interior scene: Tom and Nick stand conversing near the couch while Daisy, petite and vibrant in white, laughs at some private joke. Jordan, elegant and cool, regards Nick with detached curiosity. A servant brings in cocktails on a tray. Crisp evening light glows through the French windows; the mood is flirtatious, brittle, laden with undercurrents.
ch1-scene9: A candlelit table on a flower-filled porch facing the sunset; Daisy and Jordan settle gracefully, their white dresses catching the fading light. Tom and Nick take their places opposite. The table is set in period luxury, the last golden sunlight and the flicker of candles mingling. Distant views of the bay shimmer. The mood grows uneasy with subtle interpersonal tensions.
ch1-scene10: Around the dinner table, Tom grows animated and tense, gesturing with a glass as he expounds his ideas. Daisy listens with ironic detachment, Jordan with guarded cynicism, and Nick with polite disbelief. The sunset dims, candlelight dominates, and an uneasy hush falls as uncomfortable subjects arise. A servant hovers in the background, about to answer a ringing phone.
ch1-scene11: With Tom briefly absent, Daisy leans toward Nick across the candlelit porch, her face animated and radiant as she confides in hushed tones. The porch is shadowy, with the last daylight gone and the warm flicker of candles reflected in silver and glass. Jordan listens with subtle intrigue; the servant passes through a distant doorway. Tension and intimacy fill the air.
ch1-scene12: Twilight deepens as Tom and Jordan amble together toward the mansion’s library, silhouettes against the open French doors. Daisy and Nick linger for a moment on the porch steps in the deep gloom, lit only by faint moonlight and a dim lamp inside. The air is thick with the scent of flowers and the onset of darkness. Emotions run quietly high.
ch1-scene13: On a shadowed wicker settee at the edge of the veranda, Daisy sits, head in hands, her expression fraught yet beautiful in the cool blue dusk. Nick listens intently, the night deepening around them. In the distance, garden lamps begin to glow. The mood is intimate, melancholic, and slightly tragic.
ch1-scene14: In a richly furnished crimson sitting room, Tom relaxes at one end of a stately couch, boots shining under the lamp, while Jordan, elegant with a book in her lap, reads aloud. The golden light of table lamps plays over mahogany furniture and Art Deco details. Daisy and Nick enter softly, the scene both domestic and potentially dissonant.
ch1-scene15: Tom and Daisy, resplendent in evening wear, stand side by side in a warmly lit foyer, bidding Nick farewell at the open front door. Daisy’s smile is luminous, Tom stands firm; Nick, hat in hand, is polite but pensive. The darkness outside hints at the divide between guest and hosts. The mood is polite, formal, and a little detached.
ch1-scene16: Nighttime at West Egg: Nick sits pensively on a grass roller near his modest bungalow, under the bright moon and rustling trees. Across the lawn, the silhouette of Gatsby emerges alone from the shadow of his grand mansion, hands in pockets, gazing at the starlit sky and the distant green light across the bay. The atmosphere is hushed, mystical, and haunted by longing; the night air is heavy with midsummer sounds.
ch4-scene0: A vibrant 1920s garden party scene on the sweeping lawn of Gatsby’s palatial Long Island mansion. Dozens of fashionable men and women in Jazz Age formalwear mingle between flowerbeds, sipping cocktails and laughing under bright morning sunlight. Waiters circulate with trays, and sleek automobiles are parked in the drive. The atmosphere is lively, colorful, and luxurious.
ch4-scene1: Gatsby’s lavish cream-colored car is pulled up on a gravel drive outside a modest 1920s cottage. Nick, wearing a casual summer suit, stands near his front step as Gatsby, impeccably dressed in a caramel-colored suit, balances on the dashboard, gesturing amiably. The scene is sunlit and quiet, the extravagant car contrasting with the humble house.
ch4-scene2: The glossy interior of Gatsby’s luxurious 1920s automobile; Gatsby in his caramel-hued suit drives with restless energy, while Nick, dressed in conservative attire, sits beside him observing. Through the wide windows, Long Island’s summer landscape and, later, the gritty industrial expanse of the Valley of Ashes pass by. Sunlight flickers in, and the feeling is a mix of privilege and tension.
ch4-scene3: Gatsby’s grand car speeds across the steel girders of the Queensboro Bridge in bright noon light. The city skyline looms ahead in white, sunlit masses. Other 1920s vehicles share the road—a hearse with flowers, bustling limousines. The river glints below, and the energy is that of promise and possibility.
ch4-scene4: A dim, cozy 1920s Manhattan restaurant cellar. Gatsby and Nick, both in sharp suits, are seated at a small table with Meyer Wolfshiem, a shrewd-looking man with a broad nose and distinctive cufflinks. Period décor, highballs on the table, and waiters in uniform provide a sophisticated Prohibition-era atmosphere.
ch4-scene5: The bustling lobby of a Manhattan restaurant at midday, filled with well-dressed patrons. Nick stands near a table, Tom Buchanan approaches, imposing in his riding clothes, as Gatsby quickly slips out of view. Overhead fans whir and sunlight filters from high basement windows, casting complex shadows and lending a feeling of sudden tension.
ch4-scene6: A refined outdoor tea garden at the Plaza, 1920s New York, with wicker chairs and shaded tables. Jordan Baker sits upright in a crisp white dress, recounting her story. Nick listens attentively, modern city life flowing around them. Dappled sunlight and the serenity of manicured greenery evoke distance from the city’s bustle.
ch4-scene7: A grand Southern-style home in Louisville, 1917, with broad lawns decorated in red, white, and blue banners for wartime. Young Daisy, in a white dress, sits in an open-top white roadster with a dashing army lieutenant in uniform. Jordan, in a smart plaid skirt, observes from nearby. Sunlight plays over the genteel, patriotic scene; the setting is upbeat yet intimate.
ch4-scene8: Daisy’s opulent bedroom in her family’s Louisville home, 1917. Daisy, in a luxurious flowered dress, lies dramatically across her bed, a bottle of wine in one hand and a letter in the other. Jordan, in bridesmaid attire, stands nearby, visibly concerned. Rich evening light filters through gauzy curtains; the room is a swirl of flowers, pearls, and wedding preparations.
ch4-scene9: A sun-bathed Santa Barbara beach in the early 1920s. Daisy, in a stylish white bathing dress, sits in the sand with Tom Buchanan’s head cradled in her lap. He lounges, eyes closed, his arm over his face, while she gazes at him affectionately. Couples stroll nearby, the Pacific sparkles, and the mood is tender yet tinged with irony.
ch4-scene10: A horse-drawn victoria carriage moves along a path in Central Park at sunset. Nick and Jordan ride seated together in 1920s finery: Nick in suit and fedora, Jordan in a cloche hat and silk dress. Behind them, Manhattan’s tall apartment buildings rise, their windows glowing in the twilight. Children play on the grass, and the city’s energy softens under the deepening evening sky.
ch5-scene0: A moody, rain-slicked front yard at night on Long Island, with Nick Carraway stepping out of a taxi under the faint glow of Gatsby’s fully illuminated mansion nearby. Jay Gatsby, in a dark coat, approaches across the manicured lawn. The atmosphere is charged, lights from Gatsby's house casting surreal reflections on the wet ground. The scene feels tense, with only Nick and Gatsby visible, dressed in 1920s evening wear, the night thick and silent around them.
ch5-scene1: Inside a modest, bright kitchen of Nick Carraway's bungalow, Nick stands at a telephone in his shirtsleeves, sunlight filtering through a rain-streaked window. Later, a Finnish housekeeper, dressed plainly, moves about the kitchen, while the front door opens to reveal Gatsby’s gardener in a raincoat with a lawn mower. Fresh flowers and lemons are set on a table, the air bustling with quiet preparation for guests old and new. The setting contrasts Nick’s humble home with hints of the anticipation of Daisy’s visit.
ch5-scene2: A 1920s luxury car stands in the driveway under wet lilac trees, rain softly falling. Daisy Buchanan, radiant in a three-cornered lavender hat and elegant dress, steps gracefully from the car while Nick reaches out to assist her. In the background, the lingering storm muting colors and noises. Inside, the living room is tidy yet modest, pastel furnishings catching the weak daylight. The sense is one of nervous anticipation with Daisy’s vibrant presence set against the dull rain and Nick’s understated home.
ch5-scene3: Inside Nick’s simply furnished living room, Gatsby—dressed in a white flannel suit, silver shirt, and gold tie—stands tense and pale near the mantelpiece. Daisy, sitting delicately on a stiff chair, looks both nervous and excited. Nick stands aside, observing. The lighting is diffused from the rainy windows, with an air of fragile formality. In the kitchen, the Finnish housekeeper brings in tea things and lemon cakes, further crowding the small, almost claustrophobic space with genteel, awkward hospitality.
ch5-scene4: A lush, rain-dampened backyard framed by a huge knotted tree and a view of Gatsby’s grand house in the distance. Nick stands sheltered beneath the tree, watching the sun begin to emerge after the storm, with the muddy lawn showing care from earlier gardening. Later, the living room glows softly with sunlight returning, Daisy dabs her tearful eyes by a mirror, and Gatsby, now beaming with happiness, stands aglow beside her. The mood shifts from contemplative and isolated to one of radiant renewal.
ch5-scene5: A majestic, sunlit façade of Gatsby’s mansion as Daisy, Nick, and Gatsby approach. Inside, rooms styled after Marie Antoinette and the Restoration brim with gilded furniture, luxurious silk curtains, and fresh flowers set for admiring guests. Period details—grand staircases, marble floors, Merton College library—highlight opulence and emptiness, with Daisy gazing in awe and Gatsby watching her every reaction. The tour winds through grand bedrooms and salons, with the group often small and dwarfed by the immense, exquisitely decorated spaces. The feeling is that of both spectacle and intimacy.
ch5-scene6: In a tastefully simple yet elegant master bedroom, Daisy sits at a vanity dresser, delightedly running a golden brush through her hair; Gatsby, in a relaxed but lavish dressing gown, sits nearby, head bowed with laughter. Sunlight breaks through rain-spattered windows, illuminating stacks of colorful bespoke shirts that Gatsby shows off, tossing them onto a large table in a dramatic, almost playful gesture. Nick lingers, observing the emotionally charged scene as Daisy is moved to tears, the opulent fabrics embodying Gatsby’s devotion. The mood is intimate, reflective, and emotionally intense.
ch5-scene7: A shaded study featuring dark wood, a large desk, and shelves lined with books. Gatsby stands near Daisy, showing her a framed photograph of Dan Cody and newspaper clippings about her. Daisy, stylish and subdued, examines them intently. Through grand windows, rain continues over the Sound, with distant glimpses of a green light. Nick surveys the room’s tokens of Gatsby's past, while the telephone sits prominently. The scene is quietly revealing, filled with nostalgia and growing intimacy amid the softly lit, secluded space.
ch5-scene8: In the golden lamplight of Gatsby’s elegant music room, Daisy and Gatsby lounge together on a luxurious couch, their positions intimately close. Ewing Klipspringer, self-conscious in a ‘sport shirt’ and duck trousers, sits at an upright piano, hands uncertain on the keys. The parquet floor gleams, and the room’s glamorous decor echoes the Jazz Age’s exuberance. Outside, evening approaches with distant thunder and rain, while all the house’s lights shine brightly. The atmosphere is dreamy, full of hope, uncertainty, and a bittersweet magic.
ch5-scene9: At the marble steps outside Gatsby’s mansion, Nick pauses for a last look back from beneath a shared umbrella, rain coming down in twilight. Inside, through glowing windows, Gatsby and Daisy are seen together, lost in each other, illuminated by the opulent house’s golden light. The wet driveway reflects the mood: wistful, romantic, and quietly melancholy as Nick turns away, an observer receding from a private, enchanted world. The scene captures the emotional aftermath and the grandeur of Gatsby’s dream.
ch8-scene0: A restless Nick Carraway sits on his bed in a dimly lit, simply furnished 1920s bedroom, early morning light faint in the window. Moments later, Nick hurries across the dew-covered lawn toward the grand, shadowy entrance hall of Gatsby’s mansion, where Gatsby stands wearily against a table. The hall is large and opulent but draped in darkness and a sense of abandonment; both men are in period pajamas and robes, looking tired and drawn.
ch8-scene1: Inside the extravagant but dimly lit drawing-room of Gatsby’s mansion, Nick and Gatsby search through dusty, unused rooms for cigarettes. Furniture is covered, shadows in the corners, and dust motes hang in the air. The two men open the French windows, then sit together looking out into the lingering darkness, their figures small against the vast backdrop of wealth and emptiness. Gatsby appears exhausted; Nick’s concern is visible.
ch8-scene2: A handsome young Gatsby, in a World War I officer’s uniform, stands in awe in the grand, tastefully decorated foyer of Daisy’s Louisville family mansion. The 1910s-1920s setting is ornate with marble floors, lush carpets, and high ceilings, bathed in warm lamplight. Daisy, radiant in a light dress, flits upstairs, the air thick with parties and the unseen presence of fashionable guests from her privileged world.
ch8-scene3: On a moonlit, elegantly furnished front porch in Louisville, young Gatsby and Daisy sit side by side on a wicker settee. She gleams in a silver evening gown, her hair shining, while Gatsby, in an officer’s uniform, looks at her with awe. The porch glows with starlight and the distant sounds of a party; Daisy is poised and ethereal, Gatsby breathlessly attentive; the scene is full of yearning and Jazz Age glamour.
ch8-scene4: A warmly lit, tastefully decorated drawing-room in Louisville, with a fireplace blazing softly. Gatsby, still in uniform, sits closely with Daisy in his arms on a plush sofa; she is flushed and softly dressed, her head resting against him. Outside the window red and orange leaves blow; there’s a tranquil, wistful air of impending farewell.
ch8-scene5: A montage-style scene: Gatsby in military dress at the front lines in France, then in academic robes amid the historic, ivy-clad stone halls of Oxford University. Various wartime and postwar settings, all featuring Gatsby isolated among strangers or crowds, Daisy’s letters clutched in his hand, surrounded by the energy and chaos of the late 1910s and 1920s.
ch8-scene6: A dazzling Louisville ballroom during the Jazz Age, filled with affluent guests in tuxedos and sparkling flapper dresses, an orchestra playing as couples swirl on a polished floor. Daisy is amid a group of suitors, dresses and beads glittering, her features simultaneously joyous and distracted, lost in a haze of luxury, restless energy, and uncertainty.
ch8-scene7: Morning breaks into the opulent but faded interiors of Gatsby’s now-quiet mansion as Gatsby and Nick move from window to window, suffused in pale, golden dawn light. The two pass through high-ceilinged rooms and out onto the wide porch, breathing in the crisp air; in the garden, a gardener stands waiting, rake in hand, ready for autumn, as Gatsby looks on thoughtfully in his pink suit.
ch8-scene8: Nick and Gatsby stand atop the broad steps of Gatsby’s mansion bathed in cool morning sunlight; Gatsby, striking in a bright pink suit, stands still as Nick descends the steps. The autumn air is evident in fallen leaves on the lawn. The gardener lingers at a distance. There is a sense of parting and sadness.
ch8-scene9: A 1920s Manhattan office with high windows, sunlight glancing off pile of papers and a rotary phone. Nick Carraway, looking fatigued in business dress, sits behind a cluttered desk. The space feels impersonal and busy, skyscrapers looming outside. Nick’s moment of weariness and isolation is palpable.
ch8-scene10: In the same sunlit Manhattan office, Nick sits at his desk with the telephone to his ear. Jordan Baker, at another location, is imagined in a glamorous 1920s suit, seated elegantly by a phone table in a hotel or club room. Both appear tense, their faces reflecting cool detachment and emotional distance as they handle the awkward call.
ch8-scene11: At night, a grim crowd gathers in front of Wilson’s bleak garage in the Valley of Ashes. Men and women in modest attire huddle under harsh electric lights, faces tired and sorrowful. Inside, Catherine arrives distressed, supported by bystanders near the sparse, dingy office. The tragic, industrial landscape is filled with smoke and shadows.
ch8-scene12: Inside Wilson’s cramped, dirty office, George B. Wilson sits hunched and broken on a battered couch, rocking back and forth. Michaelis and a few men keep vigil with him through the small hours. The space is cluttered and work-stained, an exposed bulb overhead; outside, crowds ebb and flow, then fade as dawn approaches and blue light fills the window.
ch8-scene13: Michaelis opens a bureau drawer under the pale blue morning light, withdrawing a silver-braided dog-leash. Wilson, wild-eyed and desperate, fixates on him in the cluttered garage office. At the window, Wilson’s profile is silhouetted before the gigantic, pale billboard of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg’s eyes, ominous in the breaking dawn. The scene is tense, surreal, haunted.
ch8-scene14: With pale daylight now flooding the office, Michaelis stretches in exhaustion as another man arrives; simple breakfast things are laid out. Outside, the Valley of Ashes looks even more bleak and colorless in the cold morning. Wilson is subdued, staring vacantly; an atmosphere of numb grief hangs over the scene.
ch8-scene15: A haggard, determined George Wilson trudges along empty rural roads under a hot midday sun. Passing through small 1920s towns, he is glimpsed by boys on bicycles and garage workers, his suit disheveled, his face haunted. The road winds through flat, dusty landscapes as the hours drag toward afternoon.
ch8-scene16: In Gatsby’s muted, echoing mansion, Gatsby, now dressed in a stylish 1920s bathing suit, grabs a brightly colored pneumatic mattress from the shadowy garage. Gatsby’s butler and chauffeur are present, both in smart livery, helping him prepare as he instructs them crisply. Afternoon light slants into the garage and spills across the empty rooms; there is a palpable sense of finality and anticipation.
ch8-scene17: A sunlit, almost empty swimming pool lies surrounded by yellowing trees in the lush estate. Gatsby, in a period bathing suit, carries a mattress through the garden, with the butler and chauffeur watching from a distance. The scene is serene but tinged with melancholy: the bright sun is at odds with the looming tragedy.
ch8-scene18: By the pool in Gatsby’s estate, a tragic tableau: Gatsby’s body floats lifelessly on a mattress in the shimmering blue water, faint ripples spreading. Leaves swirl on the surface. On the pool’s edge, the butler, chauffeur, gardener, and Nick rush forward with panic and shock; a deadly silence pervades, starkly emphasized by bright afternoon sunshine. In the grass some distance away, the body of George Wilson lies where he fell.
ch6-scene0: A bright summer morning outside the grand entrance of Gatsby’s lavish 1920s mansion. The sunlight gleams on stone steps and manicured lawns. A nattily dressed young reporter in a rumpled suit and hat stands facing Jay Gatsby, a striking man in an immaculate suit. Gatsby is perfectly composed at his front door, the air of wealth and enigma surrounding him. A butler waits discreetly in the background. The mood is slightly tense, with curiosity and polite formality.
ch6-scene1: A remote shore of Lake Superior in the early 1900s. The afternoon sun shimmers on the water. A teenage James Gatz, gangly and earnest, wears a torn green jersey and canvas trousers as he stands barefoot on pebbly sand, watching the sleek white yacht of Dan Cody anchored close to shore. The yacht looks stately, with polished rails and crew moving on deck. The scene is filled with a sense of longing and possibility.
ch6-scene2: The opulent deck of Dan Cody’s yacht, the Tuolomee, late afternoon. A mature, florid Dan Cody dressed extravagantly in a yachting suit stands with his young protégé Gatsby, now smart in a blue coat and white duck trousers with a yachting cap. The glint of polished brass fittings and mahogany railings emphasize the luxury. Other crew members are visible at a respectful distance. The water glitters beyond, and the air is one of adventure, indulgence, and slightly dangerous glamour.
ch6-scene3: A foggy night on the luxurious yacht at anchor in Boston harbor, with lantern light reflecting off the water. Ella Kaye, an elegantly dressed woman in fashionable early 1900s attire, stands apart on deck, her face partly obscured by shadow. James Gatz and Dan Cody are also present, but a sense of tension and foreboding fills the deck. The atmosphere is somber and mysterious, with the city lights of Boston faint in the background.
ch6-scene4: Inside Gatsby’s lavish, dimly-lit study, the decor Art Deco and opulent. Nick Carraway sits pensively with Gatsby, who is shaded by the warm light of a brass desk lamp. Heavy drapes, gleaming shelves, and the famous portrait of Dan Cody hang in the background. The mood is intimate and confidential as Nick listens thoughtfully to Gatsby’s revelations.
ch6-scene5: A sunlit Sunday afternoon on the grand porch of Gatsby’s house. Tom Buchanan, large and imposing in equestrian riding clothes, stands with Mr. Sloane and Mrs. Sloane, both attired for horseback in smart 1920s riding outfits. Gatsby greets them politely at the door, wearing a light summer suit, while Nick hovers nearby, looking surprised. Horses tethered in the drive are visible, and the overall mood is one of social awkwardness with undertones of tension.
ch6-scene6: Late afternoon on Gatsby’s spacious front porch with Art Deco columns. Mr. Sloane and Mrs. Sloane stand close in private conversation, while Tom Buchanan stands apart, frowning and brooding. Nick lingers nearby, hands in pockets. Gatsby is glimpsed just within the open door, hurriedly preparing to follow. The light is warm, casting long shadows, and the atmosphere is uneasy and expectant.
ch6-scene7: Gatsby’s extravagant garden at twilight, strings of lights glowing over a vibrant 1920s party. Hundreds of guests in tuxedos and flapper dresses mingle and dance. Daisy Buchanan arrives on Tom’s arm, both elegantly dressed, Daisy in shimmering evening wear. Gatsby, debonair and hopeful, stands near Nick and Jordan Baker, introducing them to various celebrities. The scene buzzes with color, glamour, and an undercurrent of social malaise.
ch6-scene8: Warm summer night on the steps of Nick’s modest house next door. Daisy, glowing in an evening dress, sits close with Gatsby, who looks adoringly at her. Nick, dressed in a tuxedo, stands discreetly in the garden, watching over them as instructed. The party lights twinkle across the lawn, and in the background, guests continue to stroll and laugh. The mood is intimate and bittersweet.
ch6-scene9: Inside Gatsby’s festively decorated ballroom, a supper table crowded with boisterous guests in glittering evening attire. Daisy and Nick sit together, Daisy looking distracted and bored. Miss Baedeker, flushed and disheveled, slumps against a friend, her dress damp. Doctor Civet, in a tuxedo with a physician’s reserve, sits nearby, while a group of women chatter and laugh tipsily. The centerpiece is extravagant, the lighting golden but slightly harsh, emphasizing a sense of superficial merriment and discomfort.
ch6-scene10: Soft moonlight bathes a corner of Gatsby’s lush garden. Daisy, in a glowing evening gown, stands with Nick as they discreetly watch the movie director and his Star, who sit beneath a blooming white-plum tree, faces almost touching in a moment of cinematic intimacy. The lawn beyond glitters with lamplight, and distant guests are visible. The scene conveys enchantment and wistful detachment.
ch6-scene11: Night on the marble steps at the front of Gatsby’s illuminated mansion. Daisy sits draped in her fur collar, her expression troubled, next to Tom who stands with arms crossed, face stern. Nick is with them, hands in his lap. Light spills out from the open door, and the drive is shadowy, guests departing in elegant cars. The mood is tense, reflective, and melancholy.
ch6-scene12: Deep night in Gatsby’s garden, the remnants of the party—crushed flowers and favors—litter the lawn. Gatsby stands forlorn, hatless, talking to Nick beneath the shadowed trees. Gatsby’s face is drawn and tired but animated as he speaks earnestly. The mansion looms in the background, windows dark. The air is cool and heavy with the scent of summer’s end; the mood is pensive, melancholic, and yearning.
ch6-scene13: A romantic, memory-soaked sidewalk in Louisville, bathed in silvery autumn moonlight. The trees are bare, leaves scattered on the pavement. A young Gatsby, dapper in a suit, and Daisy, radiant in a white dress, stand close together, their faces illuminated and intensely focused on each other. The world is hushed and dreamy, suggesting the magic and fatefulness of first love.
ch7-scene0: A grand Long Island mansion looms in shadow, its windows dark for the first time. On the stone steps, Nick Carraway stands facing a closed door, behind which a grim-faced, unfamiliar butler glares suspiciously. The scene is noon-bright but somber, jazz-era style clothing: Nick with a straw boater and summer suit, the butler in somber livery. The mood is tense and uncertain, the house itself exuding a sudden air of mystery.
ch7-scene1: Inside Nick Carraway's modest West Egg living room, sunlight streaming through a window, Nick sits by a small desk holding a candlestick telephone. The scene is peaceful and warmly lit, Nick in shirtsleeves and vest, a sense of quiet curiosity on his face as he listens to Gatsby's invitation. The 1920s decor is modest but tidy, evoking the atmosphere of anticipation.
ch7-scene2: A 1920s commuter train emerges from a shadowy tunnel into blinding noon sunlight, brass fixtures gleaming and straw seats nearly unbearable in the heat. Nick Carraway sits exhausted beside a perspiring woman in a white shirtwaist. Other passengers, sweating and listless, stare blankly. A conductor in blue uniform checks tickets, dabbing his brow, the mood heavy and oppressive.
ch7-scene3: The interior of a wealthy East Egg mansion: Nick and Gatsby, both in light summer suits, are ushered in by the Buchanans' butler. In the cool, awning-shaded salon, Daisy Buchanan and Jordan Baker recline languorously in flowing white dresses on a huge divan, the heat visible in the lazy swirl of air from oscillating fans. The room is elegant, dim, and calm, with a strong sense of Jazz Age luxury.
ch7-scene4: A softly lit, elegant salon: Daisy Buchanan sits upright on the couch in a white dress as a nurse enters, leading Daisy’s small daughter in a light frock. Jordan Baker lounges on the couch; Gatsby and Nick stand nearby, visibly surprised. The pristine 1920s decor, lush draperies, and gentle sunlight create a dreamy, slightly formal mood.
ch7-scene5: On the wide stone veranda of the Buchanan mansion, Tom leads Gatsby and Nick outside into glaring summer sun. Beyond the shade, the green Sound glistens, a single small sailboat in the distance. Gatsby stands at the rail gesturing grandly across the bay, Tom beside him, and Nick a pace behind. The garden is orderly but heat-wilted, with all men in summer suits and boater hats. The air is shimmering with heat.
ch7-scene6: Five figures gather at a heavy table in a darkened, elegant 1920s dining room. Tom, Daisy, Gatsby, Nick, and Jordan sit with glasses of ale before them, the drawn curtains casting the room in blue-black shadow, cut by slivers of sunlight. Daisy looks distraught, on the verge of tears; the others are tense with suppressed emotion. The table is set with silver and fine china, but the mood is anxious and unsettled.
ch7-scene7: Outside the Buchanan mansion in the searing afternoon heat, Daisy stands beside Gatsby, hand on his coat. Nearby, Tom, Nick, and Jordan—each restless, in stylish summer attire—stand among shining cars on a white gravel drive. Daisy and Gatsby prepare to get into Tom’s coupé while Tom, Jordan, and Nick pile into Gatsby’s yellow car. Small tight hats and metallic capes for the women, a sense of simmering tension as the party splits. The sunlight is blinding.
ch7-scene8: Inside the plush interior of a bright yellow Rolls-Royce, Tom nervously mans the wheel, Nick sits tensely beside him, and Jordan rides in the middle, all three visibly uncomfortable in the heat. The car barrels along a sun-drenched road past parched fields and the looming billboard of Dr. T.J. Eckleburg. Their expressions are taut, reflecting suspicion and agitation. The clothing is crisp but rumpled from travel and heat.
ch7-scene9: At a shabby garage surrounded by gray ash heaps, Tom, Jordan, and Nick step out beside the yellow car. George Wilson emerges looking pale and sickly, shaded in the doorway; sweat stains his thin shirt. Myrtle is glimpsed at an upstairs window, face filled with anxious jealousy. The afternoon light is glaring and hazy, accentuating the bleakness. The looming Dr. T.J. Eckleburg billboard watches overhead.
ch7-scene10: The yellow car whips through industrial outskirts and over bridges as Tom accelerates, a strained Nick and Jordan in the seats beside him. Flashes of the blue coupe with Daisy and Gatsby are seen ahead. The city skyline looms in the haze, the spidery girders of the elevated tracks throwing sharp black shadows. The feeling is frantic, driven by heat and emotional turmoil.
ch7-scene11: A stifling, elegantly furnished hotel parlor: Gatsby, Daisy, Tom, Jordan, and Nick assemble inside, Daisy at the mirror adjusting her hair, Tom unwrapping a whisky bottle, and Gatsby tense near the window. Four o’clock sunlight filters through sheer curtains; droplets of sweat glisten on foreheads. Jordan lounges on a chair, period hats and dresses lending a chic air despite the fraught mood. The atmosphere is heavy with anticipation; a sense of confrontation looms.
ch7-scene12: All five sit or stand tensely in the grand but overheated parlor of the Plaza. Tom leans forward, flushed and stern; Daisy sits at the center, visibly distressed, eyes flicking between Tom and Gatsby; Gatsby stands rigidly, painfully controlled. Jordan and Nick watch warily from the side. Ice and bottles rest on a silver tray, hats discarded. The setting is ornate 1920s, but stifling, with emotions about to boil over.
ch7-scene13: Daisy, sobbing by the table, presses a handkerchief to her eyes as Tom stands glowering, Gatsby’s face stricken. Jordan and Nick withdraw toward the periphery of the lavish, stuffy room, both anxious and awkward. Daisy's white dress and Gatsby’s pink suit stand out against rich damask wallpaper and gilded furniture, illuminated by weak afternoon light.
ch7-scene14: Outside the grand entrance of the Plaza Hotel, Tom, Nick, and Jordan board Tom’s blue coupé in the fading twilight. Tired, emotionally exhausted, their expressions are distant. Manhattan’s city lights flicker as they drive toward the dark silhouette of the Queensboro Bridge. The mood is subdued and heavy, the heat beginning to fade into dusk.
ch7-scene15: Between the gray heaps, Michaelis—a young Greek man in a work shirt—stands outside a small, humble restaurant next to Wilson’s garage. The early evening sky is gray with smoke. George Wilson looks sick and haggard inside his small office. The alley is deserted except for a couple of workmen heading home, their figures silhouetted against the dusk. The mood is weary and tense.
ch7-scene16: A tragic roadside scene: Myrtle Wilson bursts from the garage into the road, her arms flailing, as a large yellow car rockets past, vanishing into the gathering dusk. Michaelis stands frozen in his restaurant doorway, witnessing the accident. The road glimmers in the twilight, and the valley of ashes looms bleak and industrial. The moment is one of fractured movement and horror.
ch7-scene17: A crowd gathers around Wilson’s garage under a swinging yellow light. Myrtle’s covered body lies on a worktable inside, Tom stands at her side, anguished, while a motorcycle policeman takes notes. Wilson, wild-eyed, clings to the doorframe of his office, swaying in shock. The crowd presses forward; Nick and Jordan look stunned among the onlookers. The setting is two-tone with electric glare and deep shadow.
ch7-scene18: Tom drives his coupe through the night, Nick and Jordan silent and weary beside him. Moonlight streams onto the empty road; Tom’s face is drawn with grief, a tear visible. The car headlights carve a path through Long Island darkness, with the roadside trees appearing ghostly. The mood is haunted and somber.
ch7-scene19: The stately mansion glows softly in the midnight gloom, light spilling from second story windows. Tom, Nick, and Jordan return in the coupe and pause outside; Nick, pale and exhausted, turns away from the open door. The air is still, the night quiet except for distant cicadas. The scene is contemplative and lonely.
ch7-scene20: Near the gates of the Buchanan estate, Nick steps into the moonlit drive and encounters Gatsby in his glowing pink suit, emerging from the shadows beneath overhanging trees. The only illumination is the silver of the moon, casting deep, mysterious shadows. Gatsby stands tense, watchful, as Nick approaches, both figures isolated in the hush of midnight.
ch7-scene21: Nick walks quietly around the shadowed Buchanan estate, peering through a small window into the well-lit pantry. Daisy and Tom sit across from each other at the kitchen table, a plate of fried chicken and bottles of ale untouched between them. Tom’s hand covers Daisy’s as they quietly commune. The overall tone is hushed, intimate, and charged with an uneasy truce.
ch7-scene22: In the moonlit gravel drive, Gatsby stands alone, gazing toward the glowing windows of the Buchanan mansion. Shadows fall deep across the hedges and lawns; Gatsby is a solitary figure, hands in pockets, face anxious and resolute, a pale pink glow from Daisy’s room illuminating the upper windows. The atmosphere is heavy with longing and irrevocable loss.
ch9-scene0: A rain-soaked day outside Gatsby's grand mansion in West Egg. Police and news photographers bustle at the front door, while a rope blocks the main gate, guarded by a uniformed policeman. Curious children peep through hedges and around the pool, their faces a mix of fascination and awe. 1920s autos are parked nearby, and the mood is tense and chaotic.
ch9-scene1: Inside Gatsby's lavish drawing-room, Nick stands isolated as police, detectives, and a few officials pass through, some pausing by the covered body of Gatsby visible on a sofa or nearby. The furnishings are elegant, with jazz-age decor and heavy drapes. The light is subdued, dust motes swirling in the haze, and the loneliness is palpable on Nick’s face.
ch9-scene2: A small, tidy bedroom with a telephone placed on a writing desk. Nick, weary and anxious, holds the receiver to his ear. Sunlight slants through drawn curtains, casting long shadows. The atmosphere is tense and expectant, with a sense of loneliness in the historic setting.
ch9-scene3: Nick stands in a richly furnished hallway, phone in hand with a notepad. The butler, dignified in dark livery, offers Wolfshiem’s address on a slip of paper. Distant figures bustle in the background as the silent house looms with jazz-era opulence and an air of mourning.
ch9-scene4: Morning in Gatsby’s parlor—Nick waits anxiously as the butler hands him a letter from Wolfshiem. A thick envelope rests on a polished table, while outside, more police and reporters gather by the gates seen through the window. The ambiance is somber and heavy, the grandeur of the home contrasting with the loneliness inside.
ch9-scene5: Nick sits at his modest, tastefully decorated writing desk, the old-fashioned telephone pressed to his ear. Afternoon sunlight illuminates the quiet room, while Nick’s posture is tense from the unexpected news from the anonymous Chicago caller. The space is atmospheric with deep shadows and golden light.
ch9-scene6: The front hall of Gatsby's mansion, Henry C. Gatz—an elderly, frail man in a long, outdated overcoat—stands bewildered beside Nick. Gatz clutches a battered bag and umbrella, his eyes red-rimmed. The room is grand and welcoming but tinged with sorrow, shadows cast by ornate lamps and heavy marble. Nick consoles the old man, helping him with his coat.
ch9-scene7: Inside an opulent music-room, Gatz sits trembling on a plush chair as Nick stands nearby, offering a glass of milk on a gleaming side table. The decor is grand: polished floors, heavy curtains, and a grand piano in the corner. Later, Gatz stands by the drawing-room door, face flushed and tear-streaked, wandering into the vast hall in awe.
ch9-scene8: A stately guest bedroom with four-poster bed and embroidered covers; Henry C. Gatz sits at the bedside exhausted, his sparse grey beard in one hand, Nick standing nearby in a respectful posture. Late afternoon sun filters through drawn curtains, and the luxurious bedding contrasts the old man’s worn clothing.
ch9-scene9: A nighttime scene in Nick’s parlor, the lamplight casting a golden glow. Nick, still dressed in mourning, clutches the telephone impatiently, his face both hopeful and exasperated. There is a palpable sense of isolation and disappointment in the quiet, tastefully decorated room, with Klipspringer’s presence implied only by Nick’s tense conversation.
ch9-scene10: Nick stands inside a dim office suite with frosted glass labeled 'The Swastika Holding Company.' Wolfshiem, solemn in a dark suit, greets him at the door, the interior cluttered with 1920s period decor: heavy desks, paper-strewn tables. Stella, the beautiful secretary, eyes Nick mistrustfully from an interior door. The atmosphere is secretive and tense under the artificial light.
ch9-scene11: In a hallway lined with glossy wood and gilded picture frames, Henry C. Gatz excitedly shows Nick a worn photograph and a battered copy of Hopalong Cassidy. Gatz’s hands tremble with pride as he turns the relics toward Nick, who listens intently. The room is bright with interior lighting and the rain visible outside the windows adds a damp somberness.
ch9-scene12: A richly decorated hallway and parlor; the Lutheran minister in dark clerical attire stands near Nick and Gatz, who glance anxiously out the rain-streaked windows. Gatsby’s servants linger in the background, clustered together, and the house feels vast and silent except for the ticking clock and the patter of rain on glass.
ch9-scene13: A small rain-soaked cemetery, headstones half-hidden in mist. A black hearse is parked at the gate; Nick, Gatz, and the Lutheran minister stand solemnly by the open grave. Gatsby’s servants and the postman, soaked and subdued, cluster quietly. Owl Eyes, in his thick spectacles, rushes in last, removing his glasses to wipe them. Everyone is dressed in black, the sky overcast and the ground muddy.
ch9-scene14: Nick and Owl Eyes stand together by the wrought-iron cemetery gate as rain falls. Owl Eyes wipes his glasses, distraught, while Nick listens silently. The overcast sky, puddled road, and distant trees complete the dreary, mournful mood of a Long Island afternoon.
ch9-scene15: A bustling 1920s train station at dusk, travelers cloaked in heavy winter coats and furs gather under high-arched ceilings and ornate lamps. Steam rises from idling trains, and glamorous girls chat with friends as families bid goodbye. Suitcases, mufflers, and gloved hands are everywhere; the air is festive, filled with golden light and a nostalgic atmosphere.
ch9-scene16: Inside an old-fashioned Pullman railway car, young men and women in wool coats and cloche hats sit by frosted windows, gazing out at snow-covered fields. The lights of small towns flash past outside beneath a twinkling night sky. The air is festive; illuminated luggage racks and elegant train decor evoke a sense of adventure and nostalgia.
ch9-scene17: A surreal, moonlit West Egg street: gaudy, oversized Gatsby-esque mansions crouch under a brooding sky. In the foreground, four men in tuxedos carry a stretcher with a glamorous, bejeweled woman in a white evening gown, her hand limp. The colors and shadows are elongated; the scene is both grotesque and alluring—a Jazz Age dream in the style of El Greco.
ch9-scene18: A modestly elegant living room with autumn sunlight filtering through windows and laundry wafting on a line outside. Jordan Baker, poised and athletic, sits upright in a large armchair, dressed for golf. Nick stands nearby, mid-conversation, both visible in the soft, melancholic light. The mood is subdued and bittersweet.
ch9-scene19: An autumn afternoon on bustling Fifth Avenue: Tom Buchanan, broad-shouldered in an expensive overcoat, strides past high-end shop windows. Nick, in a classic 1920s suit, approaches him. The city is alive with well-dressed pedestrians. Shopfronts gleam behind glass and a jewelry store window shines in the midday sun, lending the scene an opulent yet tense air.
ch9-scene20: Twilight on the neglected lawn of Gatsby’s vast mansion; wild grass overtakes the driveway. Nick stands alone by the white marble steps, dimly illuminated by the glow of a distant streetlamp. The air is quiet, nostalgic, and slightly eerie; a taxicab waits at the gate in the shadows, and the palace-like house looms silent in the dying light.
ch9-scene21: Late at night, under a bright moon, Nick stands on the white steps of Gatsby’s shuttered mansion. An obscene word is scrawled in red brick on the lowest step, illuminated by moonlight. Nick solemnly wipes it away with his shoe, then walks down to the deserted beach and sinks into the sand, gazing out over the Sound and distant, flickering lights of ferryboats. The scene is hushed, lonely, and reflective.
